statement.markdown:
You are given an array of non-overlapping intervals `intervals` where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]` represents an interval. You are also given a new interval `[new_start, new_end]` that needs to be inserted into the intervals.

Merge all overlapping intervals to include the new interval and return the updated list of non-overlapping intervals that cover all intervals in the input.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer `n`, the number of intervals.
The next `n` lines contain two integers each, representing the start and end of an interval.
The next line contains two integers, representing the start and end of the new interval to be inserted.
statement.outputFormat:
The first line contains an integer `m`, the number of merged intervals.
The next `m` lines contain two integers each, representing the start and end of a merged interval.

constraints.description: The start of the new interval.
The end of the new interval.

examples.explanation: The new interval [2,6] overlaps with [1,3] and [6,9] and they are merged.

hints.text: The problem involves merging intervals. Think about how you can use the start and end values of the intervals to determine overlaps.
Consider iterating through the existing intervals and comparing their start and end points with the new interval to find overlaps.
The key insight is to identify that the intervals can be sorted by their start value, and then iterated through to merge any overlapping intervals, including the new one.

editorial.title: Insert Interval Problem Analysis
editorial.intuition: The problem requires merging a new interval with a list of existing intervals. A key observation is that we can partition the existing intervals into three parts: those that come before the new interval, those that overlap with it, and those that come after it.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking every interval to see if it overlaps with the new interval, and if so, merging them. This could result in a lot of repeated work and is likely to be slow, with a time complexity of O(n^2) or worse.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves making a single pass through the intervals, maintaining a running list of non-overlapping intervals. This is achieved by iterating through the intervals and appending them to either a 'left' list (if they come before the new interval), or merging them with the new interval if they overlap.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), as we are creating new lists to store the result.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that it correctly handles three cases: intervals that come before the new interval, intervals that overlap with it, and intervals that come after it. By maintaining a running minimum and maximum for the new interval, we ensure that all overlapping intervals are merged correctly.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with test cases reveals that the optimal solution correctly handles edge cases such as an empty list of intervals, or a new interval that is completely contained within an existing interval.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle the case where the new interval is completely contained within an existing interval.
Not maintaining a running minimum and maximum for the new interval, leading to incorrect merging of overlapping intervals.
Not correctly partitioning the existing intervals into three parts: those that come before, those that overlap with, and those that come after the new interval.

functionSignature.java: public List<List<Integer>> insert(int[][] intervals, int[] newInterval);
functionSignature.python: def insert(self, intervals: List[List[int]], newInterval: List[int]) -> List[List[int]]:
